speaker: interviewer
text: We're in Sutton, Surrey, England. And this is tape 3. We were talking about the decision point on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: The point. As I say, I-- I live with that moment all my life. But coming back to that period, it's tiring. My aim was to go back to Lwow and to-- to-- to-- to meet my mother. Now, I didn't know where I was. I jumped out of the cattle train. And as the train was slowing down, they were shooting. And, uh, somebody-- I saw a friend of mine shot on the spot. And I was just hiding. There were potato flowers, uh, growing high, and I was just hiding, uh, until the train moved away. And the only way I knew where-- I didn't know where I was. I just went back in the opposite direction where the train came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it dark at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, it was dark. It was night. And, um, I was stopped by a-- a-- a railway man, and he searched me. And, uh, my aunt gave me some money-- um, uh, dollars. And he even took off my earrings, and he examined me very thoroughly-- whether I have anything in hiding. [INTERVIEWER COUGHS] And he had a good day, because it was a very busy time. At that time were many trains. And he was-- usually what they used to do is to take any valuables, and they take you to the police station. But he must have been-- [INTERVIEWER COUGHS] --busy, and he was too tired. And he just didn't-- let me go after that. I was barefooted. And after three days without food, you can imagine, I was just-- covered with a shawl. And by that time, the daybreak was already coming on. There was a daybreak. And I saw women, uh, coming with produce to the market. And I just joined a group-- didn't talk, covered up, and walked with them, because I knew they will come to a town. And I entered Lwow. Lwow, at that time, was still open. The ghetto was open. They were closing after that, but it was open. And I knew the address where my aunt lived, and I went there. And I came into the room. And, uh, I met my aunt and her family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Funny enough, you know, I don't remember exactly-- but many years later-- many, many years later, I met in London somebody who was a friend of the family. And we didn't talk. Just by chance said, don't you remember? I was in the room when you arrived. So she was my witness, you know? She said, you came with big bla-- black eyes-- brown eyes-- and very frightened, and barefooted. So this-- then I went-- of course, I was dressed by my aunt. And-- and I went to see my father. Because-- as he was in the Janowska with my uncle, they used to take the columns to work every day from the concentration camp, at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I managed to give him some food. I was caught by the Ukrainian, who was going to take me to the Janowska because I went too far, too near the column. But again, chance-- he let me go back. You know, when one talks about survival, you think you could be clever, you could be this-- it was only shikzal. It was only fate-- coincidences. I was very inexperienced-- very naive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is how it happened. You know? I didn't plan it. I didn't design it. Now I look back at it, the timing was perfect. But at that time, I didn't. So I saw my father, and then I came back to my aunt. And I-- Do you remember what it was like, seeing your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. You can imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there were other people there, too, who recognized me, and they were just weeping. You know, men, grown up, broken down. Because they probably, in camp, knew more than I knew what was happened to these people, because in the camp, they already knew what was the fate of these people. Whereas I was-- didn't realize. I mean, there were rumors. When I came to this family, the same evening, there was a man there who survived. And I met him after that. He came back with a postcard. Because his wife and child were taken in an aktion previously in Lwow. He paid 20 zloty-- I don't know how much for it. And he brought the card to show that the people write, that we are well off, we are looked after.

speaker: interviewer
text: People were holding onto hope against hope-- anything. So somebody prefabricated or something, and he brought the card to reassure everybody that it's all right, you know? That they are working, and they are well looked after, and they are all right. People were-- just wanted to believe in something, in some kind of hope. So this is how it was going on between. So when I came back, I decided I'm going back to Sadowa Wisznia. Now, it was very difficult, you know? You were not allowed to use train. But again, I took off my-- my band, and I traveled-- I felt like an international spy. And I came to Sadowa Wisznia again-- It was a terribly risky thing to be doing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and you just did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: --but were living with fear and risk all the time. And I arrived to the station, and I walked. Because the station was about one and a half-- nobody recognized me, and I came. What happened in Sadowa Wisznia-- this was about, I don't know, a week or 10 days after that-- they were collecting the people who survived, like my mother and some other units who were in hiding and were after-- they were not taken on the train. So they were collecting these people, and they were then, uh, taking them to a ghetto in Jaworow. You see, they were collecting the people who survived from these little towns, where it was-- the town was made-- was going to be Judenfrei. So they were making these towns Judenfrei, the little towns, and grouping the residue-- the people who came out of the-- who were not taken in the Final Solution, the aktion-- and putting them into a ghetto. Our district, Jaworow, was the ghetto where they were taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were waiting to go to Jaworow, because they were going-- there was a date-- I don't remember-- a week or 10 days-- to go to Jaworow. And I was staying with my mother. You can imagine how-- when I met my mother, what kind of things there was.

speaker: interviewee
text: She told me that she went to our flat, which was straightaway-- you know, Ukrainians from villages came and robbed every-- and she found a photograph of me. She had a little photograph from school documents, and she kept it. And we were together, uh, living in, again, sort of-- they reduced the ghetto area. It wasn't enclosed, but the area-- so the three or four houses where these people lived together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when are we talking about, at the moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're talking November or end of October, 1942. You know, then. And I was walking with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my, uh, headmaster, a Polish headmaster that I had passed us by. And he said to my mother, in Polish, what is this child doing still here? Why don't you do something? What is she doing-- So she said, well, what can I do? Can you help me? So he said, well, come in the evening. We'll talk. And my mother, who has never, all during that period-- didn't show any initiative, didn't have any energy, was a completely defeated, sweet, loving, caring, soft, genuine-- went over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he gave her-- uh, she started working on forming documents for me to survive. Now, in bigger towns you could have bought false document. In our town, we didn't have any contact. Nobody knew about it. We didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: So he gave her an empty certificate, a school certificate. You know, when you are a child, you go-- you had reduced fare, like here, as a student. So he had these blanks. And he gave her this blank with a stamp. And she had one document.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then she went to this Olga Demczyzszak, who was the girl that we used to have this exchange over the wall. And Olga Demczyzszak gave her a birth certificate, which was issued under the Russian occupation, which she didn't consider it a real document, you know, because it was under the Russian occupation. So she didn't consider it. Nonetheless, she gave-- without any money, neither the headmaster-- without any rewards, gave her her birth certificate. So there was my mother, with a birth certificate in the name Olga Demczyzszak and a form. And she had this little photograph that she picked up when she went to look at the flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she stuck the photograph on this document. She filled it in as Olga Demczyzszak. And so I had two documents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she-- I can't get over-- the more I think about it, the more remarkable it is, how she managed to do it. We had a family that she was friendly with, Polish people. He was a-- a postman-- delivered post. And they used to shop in our place, and she used to know it. And she went over to her, to this woman. Because this-- she was in contact with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this woman had a daughter my age group who worked about 60 kilometer-- in Tomaszow, a-- a different area. And she wanted her daughter to come back to live at home. So my mother went over to her, and she said, why don't you take my daughter and bring Zosia--her daughter was Zosia-- Zosia back? And this woman agreed. So there was I, for-- with a situation where my mother did all the organizing. And it came to say good-bye. And this is another moment which I find unbearable, even to talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I knew that she needed me. I knew that she needed a support. [DRINKS] I had treatment and psychoanalysis and psychotherapy. And when everybody says, well, surely, as a mother, you know, she was easy in her mind, if she knew that you were going to life. She wanted you to have-- I know what it means to be in the last stage, where you are facing death or-- you don't have very many noble ideas. You just need comfort. And you're like an animal. You just want-- And I could have provided to her. We could have gone together. And I didn't want to go, after all that. And she tried to persuade me. She would say  that, look, if we are in a cage-- we're going into a ghetto-- we'll be closed in-- I can't help you, and you can't help me. But if you are outside, you've always managed to help. You've always showed resourcefulness. You will be able to help me more outside than inside. There, again, I don't know how much of this was her persuasion and how much was this instinct for life. And I'm still not sure which of the two was the prevalent. I remember we walked back and forwards, between the house of this woman, and I didn't want to say goodbye. On the last day, she didn't even answer my door. She didn't want to see me-- [DRINKS] my mother. And I went with this woman. It was beginning of November, already frosty cold. We walked, because we didn't have-- we didn't have any facilities to travel. So we walked. This was about-- quite a long walk, 60 kilometers. [SNIFFS] And I arrived to this village where her daughter lived. And she introduced me there as Olga Demczyzszak. This-- I was her neighbor's daughter who came here to work. And Zosia was going to come. Now Zosia, when she met this situation, realized the danger. This woman didn't realize the danger she was in. She was naive. She didn't realize. Nobody did. But this was about-- not far from Belzec.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she knew what was going-- not exactly, but in this village somebody was shot with full documents. And so she said, mother, what have you done? So I-- and she didn't want to go back. So I was resigned to go back to Jaworow, to join my mother in ghetto, because I wasn't very-- and we were going to get ready to go back. And then, in this village, there was a man a Pole who administered a German estate. And he needed a domestic. In the last minute, he sent off to this house where I was staying, that would I come and work for him? And so I stayed in this village, together with Zosia.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was difficult for her and difficult for me. She kept quiet, but she knew the danger. He didn't know I was Jewish. He was very anti-Semitic, with the jokes I could hear. But I was there. Now my difficulty in that period was that under the German law you couldn't register without having a reregistration from my previous place-- as Olga Demczyzszak. And this something I didn't know you require, and this something I couldn't get, because Olga Demczyzszak was living there. So, for a long time, I was postponing, you know. I told the story-- this was a Polish village, I was Ukrainian-- Olga Demczyzszak was Ukrainian. So I said, well, my mother was Polish, my father was Ukrainian, and this is why I didn't agree with my father and I ran away. All the time you had to prefabricate. You had to invent-- uh, innovate all the time situation as they came. Then I said, well, I wrote to them, and they lost-- the letter was lost, and then they couldn't get it. I put it off, put it off. Unless there came a stage I couldn't find another lie. I couldn't put it off any longer. By that time, it was March 1943. I didn't know what was going on outside. I only remember that a few women came to this house and said they went to look-- I don't remember the town-- a nearby town-- where they were burning ghetto. So they went for a day outing to look at it, how it was happening. I didn't know anything about what was going on in the world outside. And I then said, well, I'll go back to Jaworow. I still thought Jaworow was there. And then again, fate-- how it can-- By the time the German finished with the Jews, they started taking local population for forced labor. And this village had to produce 60 girls to go to work in Germany. And one of the daughters of the household had to go to Germany, which was a major tragedy. Berlin was very bombarded. Germany was very dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, well, I'll volunteer. I'll go instead of you. Which they agreed. So we had to go to Lublin, to the arbeitsamt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was another sort of conducive situation, because the arbeitsamt had a demand from Berlin, from a [GERMAN], for a domestic. And the girl who was going to go there was bribing him not to go to Berlin, because at that time Berlin was very bombarded. [CREW COUGHS] So this man at the desk said, fine-- this will solve his problem. It solved the problem that the daughter could stay with the family. The man in the arbeitsamt could send off somebody to Berlin instead of the girl who was feeding him with bribing him not to go. And there was I, finding myself with all the documents to go to Germany, to Berlin. I had an address-- Wallen-- Wallenbergstrasse 73-- and Diplom-Ingenieur Lang, who had a demand for the domestic to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and did this suit your-- I mean, were-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. This was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --your needs as much as anything could--

speaker: interviewee
text: --this was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: --a way out.

speaker: interviewee
text: --salvation, in a way. Because in Germany, they were very accurate and very ex-- very-- very pedantic. But they couldn't recognize my Jewish nose from an Aryan-- from any other. They had a lot of false-- forced labor from all over-- from France, from Poland. So they-- first of all, I had a new set of documents now. So they didn't need my-- my-- my document from Sadowa Wisznia. So I was then out of this danger into a new situation, which I didn't know. And I passed-- and I was quite cocky by that time, in a way. I thought, well, I solved one problem. I-- I'm going forward. And I passed through Warsaw. Because I had to change. Now, normally, these forced labor groups-- we used to go in a trainload, in a group of 60, 100. I had the individual demand, an individual ticket, an individual-- Now, I've never traveled as far-- Lemberg was the sort of-- Lwow was the biggest town I've ever visited. And I remember I had a long coat. I didn't have a suitcase. I had a bundle. And I had a long, grey coat. And I was going through to Berlin. The address was to go to an arbeitsamt. And I was going through Warsaw, changing the trains. And I had to spend the night on the station. And this was already April-- beginning-- end of March, April. This was the most dangerous time to be in Warsaw. This was the Warsaw ghetto burning. I didn't know about the Warsaw ghetto burning. I already walked around when-- and they were fishing out people like this. Because from Warsaw, you know, anybody who had false documents only-- people were looking. But I-- I was ignorant of the danger. I just didn't realize what was going on. Now, when I look back, I realize that this was a very dangerous spot, which I, in my ignorance, didn't know. And I found myself in a train going to Berlin. In Germany, when I changed the station, I felt-- I didn't speak, also. I knew how to speak German. I was afraid that they'd recognize in my accent that maybe I have a Jewish accent. So I pretended that I don't know. I just showed my documents, whatever they needed, as a ticket and everything. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How had you learnt-- you'd learnt German in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, yes. This was a second language. I didn't have French, which I regret, but I had German.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I went-- found myself, Saturday afternoon, in Berlin. I couldn't go to the office, because all the offices were closed. But I had the address of the family. Don't ask me--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I went to Berlin last year. And it's quite a complicated journey. I-- with all my experience of traveling, how I found myself using the U-Bahn underground trains. I just showed everyone my ticket. And somehow I found myself in this address, and I knocked at the door. They'd forgotten all about it, because it took such a long time to get that they-- anyhow, they-- they-- they realize what is happening. So I stayed over the weekend. And Monday morning, she took me to an office, which came through a sort of cleaning office-- delousing, shower, you know, cleaning. Almost the kind of process they used in concentration camp, except that I, after that, I had a number here and went to life, where the other people, uh, had-- had not. But I didn't know at that time. But I had a new set of documents, and I worked for this family. They were a very nice family. I know it sounds, uh, very-- uh, I always-- I sound controversial when I bear witness to the situation-- that not all German knew what was going on. They were all pro-Hitler. They were all patriotic. They were all very disciplined. They were all obedient. But the things which were done were done by a very small group which were trained to do it. All Germans, uh, followed the instruction. You know, it started by all kinds of gradually, gradually, uh, indoctrinating them. They all had the herrenvolk mentality. They all believed in their thousand-year Reich. They were all hoping that I will-- I mean, her preoccupation was that after the war I'll stay on, because I was a good domestic. I mean, she-- they all expected the East Slavian working for them, and they'll manage, and they'll be-- this is true. But also is true that they didn't know the extremities. They all-- when they were talking, talking about Jews being resettled back where they came from. It's a very controversial point, but they didn't-- they-- The same man, next door, who lived, who was an SS man-- he came home. He never talked about it. They were very, very disciplined. They never talked about it. He was a very nice neighbor. He was a very good father. He was a good husband. You know, there was a sort of split personality. They were not, uh-- This family, for example-- I'll just give you an example. They had a modern television-- a wireless, with three loudspeakers. They could have called any international station they wanted to. But they were disciplined. They were obedient. It was verboten. It was forbidden to listen to a foreign station. As a domestic, I would know what was going on. Not once did they put on a foreign station. This is the obedience. They're following the laws.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that because they were frightened, or because they just felt it was the right thing to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, they were very obedient, before, through. And also-- a lot of it, also, later on, came-- they didn't talk about a lot of things. You know, because-- children at home and other things. You know, certain subjects were not talked-- probably they was also frightened. But they were all very patriotic and obedient. Because they didn't confront the extermination. They didn't know. They thought that these people were sent back, you know-- that they live somewhere in the camp, and they're working. There started another problem for me. Because having Ukrainian document as Olga Demczyzszak-- as I told you, Ukrainian had certain privileges-- not many. I didn't experience many. But I had to go, then, to a Ukrainian-- the Ukrainian had a sort of embassy there, in Berlin, and I had to go there and to pass a certificate that I'm Ukrainian. I thought I'll never do that. I could fib along with the Germans, you know. I could-- could-- could talk like-- But there, to pass a test for Ukrainian, I never thought I'll do it. But I did it. Again, I had to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the test involve?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they talked. They examined. I think they didn't suspect. If they would have delved a little bit deeper, they could have found out, you know. But I spoke Ukrainian. And they just didn't-- it didn't occur to them to investigate it. You know, they just saw the documents, gave me the certificate, and I came home. And I worked there for about a year, I think-- yes-- as a domestic. This was a privileged family, in a way, because mostly German were entitled-- when you had four children. But because he worked as an engineer-- and I think he must have worked in Pomerania on the doodlebugs, because he used to travel. He didn't talk about this job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had privileges and had, for one child, a domestic. They were very nice. They were trying to-- to-- as a Ukrainian, of course. And they were very pleased. I was a very good domestic. Um, they-- they-- for my birthday, she would lock herself up and make some clothes for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I had a double life all the time. And this-- and I was brought up-- my grandmother always said, you know, you should always tell the truth. The truth comes out like oil on water. And this image of oil, of water coming on the surface all the time, was with me all the time. But I just had to innovate as I went along. And it was just-- uh-- confront all the challenges as they came along. As Berlin was very bombarded, there was a system in Germany that they used to send out whole classrooms to the provinces so that-- with the teacher, so the children would have a little respite from the bombardment. And her sister was a manageress of a home like this, which is in Crossen an der Oder. And so she sent her son with me--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was her son?

speaker: interviewee
text: About six? Uh, and she sent her son with me, because she wanted to keep in family, to work for her sister in this home. So then I found myself working in Crossen an der Oder.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was that from where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, well, Crossen an der Oder is now on the borderline, I think about three hours by train-- something like this-- I don't remember exactly-- from Berlin. And I then became employed by Hitlerjugend, because this was run by Hitlerjugend. And I started by-- you know, there were six other German girls, and I started, uh, being the, uh-- the lowest in the grade and coal into-- they didn't have central heating, but they had these ovens-- carrying coal. But eventually I graduated, and I become the chief cook. [LAUGHS] And that was, uh, just one of those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the things you did in between--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, cleaning-- mostly cleaning, washing, going to the bakery, because also we cooked, but baking for the weekend. We took it to the bakery, to the town. This was a little bit outside town. But there again came another difficulty. Whereas in Berlin you had a special arbeitsamt-- sort of a-- not 'arbeitsamt'-- embassy-- Ukrainian-- here the local police-- and this is where I have the document that I will show you. That once I got, uh, a letter, soon after I arrived, that I have to register in the local police. So I-- unsuspectingly-- I went to the local police and produced my documents. By that time, I had the [INAUDIBLE] documents. And he looked at it, and he said, oh, you will be very pleased, because there is a boy from [INAUDIBLE]-- you know, this little village with Olga Demczyzszak. He is here, and he is working. So you will be very pleased to have a landsman with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I died at that moment. I thought to myself, what am I doing? How-- but there was nothing-- I didn't have freedom to travel. I couldn't leave this position to go somewhere else. I had to stay put.

speaker: interviewee
text: Being outside the town, I didn't venture very much into the town. Through the bakery where we used to deliver, there was a Polish boy, an ex-prisoner of war who worked in the bakery. So I introduced me to the Polish, uh, labor-force people, so I avoided Ukrainian. And then I just thought, well, he made this remark because he had the documents in front of me. Surely he won't remember this after many times. It's very unlikely that another coincidence-- But I lived with that fear all the time, that this boy would recognize me. This boy knew that Olga Demczyzszak was living. This-- because I knew this boy, too, from school. So he would denounce me without any hesitation. But I had to stay put. With-- I didn't have any freedom of movement. And I worked in this home until the liberation, which came in 1945, February.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about being involved with the Hitlerjugend. What-- what do you remember of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only thing I knew about it-- that from time to time we had visitors, you know, from-- in uniforms, coming to check up if everything is run. But normally, you know, the classrooms were run by teachers, normally. The class-- the children were looked after so that they could be well fed, uh, coming out of Berlin. Berlin was very bombarded. An incidence comes back to my mind now. I-- It must have been in 1944. You remember when Rome fell, because Mussolini was caught. And-- and I listened to the wireless, and Italy fell. And I must have jumped of joy with a-- with a broom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somebody must have noticed that. I was so-- not self-conscious, because I was on my own in the classroom, and she called me in for an interview. And she said, how is it that you are Ukrainian and you say you're so patriotic, and all of a sudden you're jumping of joy? So I said, I didn't listen to the wireless, I just felt happy that morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, you know, you had, all the time-- you lived, all the time, on a danger level, for all kinds of things. Something I was afraid what I-- where we sharing room, whether I say something at night. Um, all kinds of things were happening at that same time. I was cut off from every news around. Of course, I didn't read in the German news about the-- the-- the Final Solution. Um, to begin with, when I arrived, you know, Germany was still very victorious. But it was later on that-- that-- that things became to-- But to the last minute, they still believed that they have this final weapon, you know, which will solve their solution. Even-- then everything was disintegrating. They were still hoping that this doodlebug that they've got-- the-- the bomb, the something which will save them in the last minute. I don't know. I mean, I'm not giving you a political review. I'm just seeing, because a girl was there who was working when her, uh, boyfriend, or-- he was in the Air Force. So she used to have the news. She said, you will see. In the last minute, something is coming up that will save us.
